nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q1.alt: Question 1. In paragraph 1, what does Minna learn from watching Willie? Choices: A: She sees that playing music makes a crowd gather. B: She notices that the music makes others happy. C: She realizes that she wants to have a vibrato. D: She discovers that music makes her sad.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q2.alt: Question 2. What new information about Willie does the reader learn in paragraph 12? Choices: A: He is more than he seems. B: He is a generous person. C: He performs with a vibrato. D: He plays with something in mind.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q3.alt: Question 3. Read these sentences from paragraph 18 of the story. He peered at Minna. “It is like a light going on over your head.” What does the phrase “a light going on over your head” mean as it is used in paragraph 18? Choices: A: a new way of understanding B: something that is above you C: a way someone helps you out D: something that happens after hard work

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q4.alt: Question 4. Which statement best reflects a theme of the story? Choices: A: Having something new can make things better. B: With time and practice, a person can achieve a goal. C: A teacher can help you figure out everything you need to know. D: Playing music is fun even when you are still learning how to do it.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q5.alt: Question 5. Read these sentences from the story. “Willie is a fine musician, Minna. And he was a fine musician before he got his vibrato.” (paragraph 12) “And we will play it wonderfully. In tune. With or without a vibrato.” (paragraph 26) How do these sentences develop the story overall? Choices: A: They explain why Willie gives back Minna’s money. B: They show Minna’s eagerness to play with a vibrato. C: They show what Minna learns from Porch about Willie. D: They suggest that Minna does not need a vibrato to play well.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q6.alt: Question 6. Based on the story, which sentence best describes Minna? Choices: A: She plays the cello because she enjoys it. B: She complains about her music lessons. C: She talks with Willie about music only. D: She thinks Mozart is too difficult.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. Read these sentences from paragraph 1 of the article. A thousand years ago, chocolate was a big secret. Only a few people drank it and nobody ate it. What is the main reason that the author begins with these sentences? Choices: A: to explain that chocolate comes from the jungle B: to explain how chocolate was discovered C: to show that chocolate used to be rare D: to show how old chocolate is

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. What is the main reason the author includes the story about monkeys at the beginning of the article? Choices: A: to show that animals like chocolate B: to describe the trees where the cacao pods came from C: to explain why the farmers first tasted the cacao pods D: to show how the beans inside cacao pods are eaten

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. Read the sentence from paragraph 8 of the article. But then, some villagers noticed a delicious smell, drifting up from a pile of rotting beans. Why did the author include the phrases “delicious smell” and “rotting beans” in the same sentence? Choices: A: to show an unexpected result B: to show what people had planned C: to describe a process as it happens D: to describe a solution to a problem

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. How did the invention of the steam engine mostly affect the making of chocolate? Choices: A: It improved the taste of the cacao bean. B: It added different ingredients to chocolate. C: It changed the way cacao trees were grown. D: It provided an easier way to make chocolate.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q17.alt: Question 17. Which sentence from the article best shows how the Mayans felt about chocolate? Choices: A: “But then, some villagers noticed a delicious smell, drifting up from a pile of rotting beans.” (paragraph 8) B: “They let the beans rot for a few days under banana leaves . . . then put them out to dry in the hot sun.” (paragraph 10) C: “To avoid hiking into the jungle for pods, the farmers planted cacao trees in their own fields.” (paragraph 12) D: “When the Mayans first caught a whiff of rotting cacao beans, they knew they had found something exciting.” (paragraph 23)

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q18.alt: Question 18. How does the author organize this article? Choices: A: by showing the effects of chocolate on people around the world B: by comparing and contrasting different ways of eating chocolate C: by describing events connected to chocolate in the order they happened D: by presenting the problem with making chocolate and then giving the solution

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q25.alt: Question 25. How does paragraph 5 relate to paragraph 4? Choices: A: Paragraph 5 explains why Sarah feels helpless in paragraph 4. B: Paragraph 5 shows how Sarah is brave even though she is afraid in paragraph 4. C: Paragraph 5 explains why the grandfather is concerned about Sarah in paragraph 4. D: Paragraph 5 reveals how the grandfather keeps Sarah out of the danger described in paragraph 4.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q26.alt: Question 26. paragraph 7, the word “croaked” shows that Sarah Choices: A: does not want the shark to notice her B: does not want to scare the shark away C: has difficulty getting the words out D: wants to hide her feelings

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q27.alt: Question 27. How is Sarah helped by her previous swimming experience? Choices: A: She knows how to get in the water without disturbing the shark. B: She knows which supplies to bring with her on the boat. C: It helps her to pretend that she is the shark. D: It helps her to feel calm in the water.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q28.alt: Question 28. Which detail best shows that the stories about sharks being killers are wrong? Choices: A: “But he was no fool and knew there were always risks when you were near any wild animal.” (paragraph 2) B: “If the shark comes toward us, just stay calm and don’t wave your hands or feet in front of him.” (paragraph 6) C: “Now she knew a shark was below her.” (paragraph 8) D: “The tiger shark rested on the bottom not ten feet from them.” (paragraph 12)

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q29.alt: Question 29. How does paragraph 12 contribute to the rest of the story? Choices: A: It shows the reason for the change in a character. B: It gives important details about the story's setting. C: It gives new details about the shark’s condition. D: It shows why people are able to get close to sharks.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q30.alt: Question 30. Which sentence describes how Sarah’s grandfather feels about sharks in the ocean? Choices: A: He thinks sharks will never harm humans who swim with them. B: He respects sharks as wild animals and is careful around them. C: He knows sharks are smarter than humans and can sense fear. D: He admires sharks’ ability to eat almost anything they find.

nysed-ela-2016-g4-mc-q31.alt: Question 31. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? Choices: A: Sharks can eat boards and even nails. B: Sharks depend on their strong sense of smell. C: Sarah notices that the shark is bigger than average. D: Sarah is nervous but she is prepared to swim with the shark.
